title: Category accounting credits the requested amount instead of the net amount actually received
description:
Root cause & mechanism
The protocol’s category token accounting can desynchronize from real on-chain token balances because deposit and withdrawal logic assumes a 1:1 relationship between the amount requested/recorded and the amount that is actually present and spendable in the category’s canonical associated token account (ATA).

Two concrete mechanisms create this desynchronization:

1) Gross-amount accounting on Token-2022 fee/deflationary transfers
In deposit_category_tokens, the program transfers amount_in_base_units via a Token-2022 CPI (token_2022::transfer_checked) and then blindly credits internal accounting with the same gross amount:
- category.unallocated_tokens_left += amount_in_base_units

For Token-2022 mints with the Transfer Fee extension enabled (TransferFeeConfig with non-zero fee), transfer_checked withholds a fee so the sender is debited amount_in_base_units while the recipient token account (category_ata) is credited only (amount_in_base_units - fee). This inflates InvestorCategoryData.unallocated_tokens_left relative to the actual increase in category_ata.amount (i.e., it records the requested amount instead of the net amount actually received). The same class of issue can occur with other deflationary mechanics such as burns or external balance reductions.

2) Non-canonical category_ata acceptance (mint/owner-only checks) causing “deposit to wrong account”
The deposit_category_tokens account constraints specify:
- associated_token::mint = mint
- associated_token::authority = category
- associated_token::token_program = token_program
but do not use init nor an explicit address = get_associated_token_address(...) check to ensure the passed category_ata is the canonically derived ATA. As described, in this configuration Anchor may only validate that the supplied token account’s mint and owner (authority) match, not that it is the canonical ATA address.

This allows a user to manually create an arbitrary Token-2022 token account owned by the category PDA (via Token-2022 InitializeAccount), pass it as category_ata, and successfully transfer tokens into that alternative account. The program still increments category.unallocated_tokens_left, but other protocol flows “inherently rely on the true canonical ATA to distribute funds”, leading to internal ledger inflation without providing liquidity where later distribution expects it.

Trigger conditions / assumptions
- deposit_category_tokens:
  - category_seed must pass require!(investor_category_seed_is_valid(&category_seed), ErrorCode::CategorySeed) (valid seeds include ["preseed","seed","institutional","vgp","founders"]).
  - amount_in_whole_sbts > 0 and conversion succeeds: amount_in_base_units = amount_in_whole_sbts * 10^SBT_DECIMALS (otherwise ErrorCode::TooManyTokensAllocated).
  - Sender has sufficient balance: require_gte!(ctx.accounts.sender_ata.amount, amount_in_base_units, ErrorCode::BalanceInsufficient).
  - No privileged role is required for deposit; code comments explicitly note “Even the master signature is not necessary. Anybody could allocate tokens to the category…”, so any unprivileged caller can create/expand the mismatch.
  - Desync can be induced by:
    - Using a Token-2022 mint that charges a transfer fee (TransferFeeConfig non-zero), and/or
    - Supplying a non-canonical token account owned by the category PDA as category_ata, and/or
    - Other balance-altering mechanics (e.g., deflationary token mechanics, burns, or direct transfers) that change category token balances without corresponding state updates.

- withdraw_category_tokens:
  - Withdrawal is performed by master (Signer with constraint master.key() == SBARTER_MULTISIG unless LOCAL_TESTING).
  - category_seed must be valid; amount_in_base_units conversion must succeed.
  - Requires:
    - category.unallocated_tokens_left >= amount_in_base_units (ErrorCode::TooManyTokensAllocated)
    - category_ata.amount.saturating_sub(category.tokens_ready_for_claim) >= amount_in_base_units (ErrorCode::TokensUnavailable)
    - category.cliff_started_at != 0 (ErrorCode::TgeNotHappened)

Impact
- Internal ledger inflation / phantom liquidity:
  InvestorCategoryData.unallocated_tokens_left (documented as “Total category allocation minus total allocations of all investors” and “Used as the upper limit of allocation when adding new investors”) can exceed the actual spendable tokens held where the system expects them (the category’s canonical ATA).

- Systemic underfunding/insolvency and denial of service:
  Downstream logic that trusts unallocated_tokens_left as an allocation/withdrawal bound (explicitly mentioned: add_investor_to_category and withdraw_category_tokens; additionally, investor claiming flows) can authorize allocations or withdrawals not fully backed by real balances. Later, when investors attempt to claim vested allocations or when withdrawals/claims execute real Token-2022 transfers, these transfers can fail due to insufficient funds in the expected category token account, effectively “bricking” or DoSing category distribution.

- Withdrawal can consume investors’ vesting tokens under desynced state:
  withdraw_category_tokens tries to protect claimable tokens by limiting withdrawals to category_ata.amount - tokens_ready_for_claim, but category_ata.amount conceptually includes:
  unallocated_tokens + tokens_ready_for_claim + vesting_tokens (allocated for future but not yet ready).
  By subtracting only tokens_ready_for_claim, the second bound treats vesting_tokens as withdrawable. If unallocated_tokens_left is disjointed from the true unallocated balance (e.g., due to transfer fees, burns, or direct transfers), the first check may no longer correctly represent the real unallocated portion; a withdrawal that passes both checks can effectively “eat into” vesting_tokens, permanently depriving investors of future allocations.

Exploitation / reproduction notes (PoC highlights)
A) Fee-on-transfer (Token-2022 TransferFeeConfig) deposit inflation
1. Use or deploy a Token-2022 mint configured with the TransferFeeConfig extension and a non-zero transfer fee.
2. Call deposit_category_tokens with:
   - a valid category_seed,
   - amount_in_whole_sbts large enough to be meaningful,
   - sender_ata funded with at least amount_in_base_units.
3. Observe that token_2022::transfer_checked debits sender_ata by amount_in_base_units but credits category_ata by only (amount_in_base_units - fee).
4. The program still executes category.unallocated_tokens_left += amount_in_base_units, creating a growing mismatch between recorded availability and real tokens.
5. Repeat to amplify the discrepancy; later allocations/withdrawals/claims may be authorized based on the inflated state and then fail or underfund investors.

B) Deposit into non-canonical token account owned by category PDA (ATA-address not enforced)
1. Generate a new Solana keypair to represent an arbitrary token account address.
2. Call Token-2022 InitializeAccount to initialize that address as a token account for the project mint, setting its owner to the category PDA.
3. Fund sender_ata with the project tokens.
4. Invoke deposit_category_tokens, passing the attacker-created token account as category_ata.
5. The checks pass because the provided token account has the correct mint and is owned by the category PDA.
6. Tokens are transferred into the alternative account, while category.unallocated_tokens_left is increased as if liquidity were added to the canonical ATA.
7. Once allocations are made, claims/withdrawal flows that pull from the canonical category ATA can revert due to insufficient balance, causing DoS/insolvency.

C) Vesting-token depletion during withdrawal under desync (illustrative numeric example)
1. Assume category_ata holds 1,000 tokens: 100 unallocated, 200 ready for claim, 700 vesting.
2. Due to an external burn/deflationary fee, category_ata.amount drops to 950 while state remains: unallocated_tokens_left = 100, tokens_ready_for_claim = 200 (vesting implied 700).
3. Call withdraw_category_tokens for 100 tokens.
4. First check passes (100 <= unallocated_tokens_left=100).
5. Second check passes (100 <= 950 - 200 = 750), because vesting tokens are implicitly counted as available.
6. Withdrawal succeeds; category_ata becomes 850. After reserving 200 ready-for-claim, only 650 remain for 700 vesting, meaning the withdrawal effectively consumed 50 tokens that should have backed vesting allocations.

Code-location anchors (if present in the inputs)
- programs/master-token-program/src/instructions/category/deposit.rs
  - Function: deposit_category_tokens
  - Validation: require!(investor_category_seed_is_valid(&category_seed), ErrorCode::CategorySeed)
  - Balance check: require_gte!(ctx.accounts.sender_ata.amount, amount_in_base_units, ErrorCode::BalanceInsufficient)
  - CPI: token_2022::transfer_checked(cpi_ctx, amount_in_base_units, SBT_DECIMALS)?
  - Faulty accounting: category.unallocated_tokens_left += amount_in_base_units
  - Account constraints of interest: category_ata uses associated_token::mint/authority/token_program but no explicit canonical ATA address enforcement (as described).

- programs/master-token-program/src/instructions/category/withdraw.rs
  - Function: withdraw_category_tokens
  - Checks:
    - require_gte!(category.unallocated_tokens_left, amount_in_base_units, ErrorCode::TooManyTokensAllocated)
    - require_gte!(category_ata.amount.saturating_sub(category.tokens_ready_for_claim), amount_in_base_units, ErrorCode::TokensUnavailable)
    - require_neq!(category.cliff_started_at, 0, ErrorCode::TgeNotHappened)
  - CPI: token_2022::transfer_checked(..., amount_in_base_units, SBT_DECIMALS)?
  - State update: category.unallocated_tokens_left -= amount_in_base_units

- State relied upon:
  - InvestorCategoryData.unallocated_tokens_left (allocation upper limit)
  - InvestorCategoryData.tokens_ready_for_claim (withdrawal bound)
  - category_ata.amount (total tokens held in the category token account)

final_verdict.reason: The “non-canonical category_ata” bypass is incorrect: Anchor’s `#[account(associated_token::mint = mint, associated_token::authority = category, associated_token::token_program = token_program)]` constraint validates that the provided account is the canonical ATA for (category, mint, token_program), so an arbitrary token account owned by the category PDA cannot be substituted. The “fee-on-transfer Token-2022” desync is also not supported by this codebase as written: the project’s mint is created via `initialize_mint` as a plain Token-2022 mint (no TransferFeeConfig/deflationary extension initialization is present), so `transfer_checked` should credit the full `amount_in_base_units`, keeping `unallocated_tokens_left` in sync with the ATA balance. This would only become an issue if the protocol intentionally deployed/used a mint with transfer-fee/deflationary extensions, which is not evidenced in the provided code/flows .

Your task is to very carefully verify the correctness of the finding, hence lowering auditors' workload by reducing false-positive findings from these tools.

    **Instructions and Requirements**:
    1. Carefully review the finding and the relevant function in the given source code. Think harder and thoroughly.
    2. Focus on verifying the given finding, even though other security issues may exist in the code.
    3. Practicality is critical and must be strictly evaluated. The finding should be practical and exploitable in a realistic context, just as how a real hacker would exploit the vulnerability.
    4. High bar for validity: The finding should be valid only if it is clear, concise, and accurate. It should be invalid if it is vague, ambiguous, or incorrect. In other words, you must be strongly convinced that the finding is correct to mark it as valid.
    5. Full context matters: The finding should be evaluated in the context of the entire codebase and its intended functionality. This includes understanding the overall architecture, design patterns, and any relevant business logic. A finding that may seem valid in isolation could be invalid when considering the full context. For example, a finding that suggests a function lacks of input validation, but is actually a part of a large business flow where validation happens downstream or upstream, may not be a valid finding.
    6. No vagueness in your analysis: use available tools to extract and find more information needed for your analysis such as searching the web or the file base.

    ** Output **
    Your output should be an instance of the AIRawFindingVerificationV2 class, which contains the following fields:
    - verdict:  A string indicating the verdict of the finding, i.e., "invalid" or "valid".
    - confidence: A string indicating the confidence level of the verdict. It can be "Strong", "Medium", or "Weak".
    - reason: A string explaining the reason for the verdict. It should be concise and clear in less than 5 sentences. If the finding needs to be further investigated, you also add the information needed to reach a conclusion.
    - model: Do not set this field. Leave it empty.

    ** Input **
    Below is the input to your verification task. Each input is delimited by tag <input_name> with the name of the input.
    *Important note*: Input 3, 4, and 5 serve as context for your analysis. However, they may not complete. Therefore, you must use available tools to search for any missing information to support your analysis.

    Input 1 - The related function where the finding is located.
    <related_function>
    pub fn deposit_category_tokens<'info>(
    ctx: Context<'_, '_, '_, 'info, DepositCategoryTokens<'info>>,
    category_seed: String,
    amount_in_whole_sbts: u64,
) -> Result<()> {
    require!(
        investor_category_seed_is_valid(&category_seed),
        crate::error::ErrorCode::CategorySeed
    );

    let Some(amount_in_base_units) =
        amount_in_whole_sbts.checked_mul(10u64.pow(SBT_DECIMALS as u32))
    else {
        msg!("You are depositing WAY too many tokens. Do you know what you're doing?");
        msg!("The instruction expects the amount to be in whole SBTs, not base units!");
        return err!(crate::error::ErrorCode::TooManyTokensAllocated);
    };

    let category = &mut ctx.accounts.category;

    // NOTE: I'm not really sure if this should be constrained in any way.
    // Even the master signature is not necessary. Anybody could allocate tokens to the category,
    // knowing that the master wallet will manage them from that point on.

    require_gte!(
        ctx.accounts.sender_ata.amount,
        amount_in_base_units,
        crate::error::ErrorCode::BalanceInsufficient
    );

    let cpi_accounts = TransferChecked {
        from: ctx.accounts.sender_ata.to_account_info(),
        to: ctx.accounts.category_ata.to_account_info(),
        authority: ctx.accounts.sender.to_account_info(),
        mint: ctx.accounts.mint.to_account_info(),
    };
    let cpi_ctx = CpiContext::new(ctx.accounts.token_program.to_account_info(), cpi_accounts);
    token_2022::transfer_checked(cpi_ctx, amount_in_base_units, SBT_DECIMALS)?;
    category.unallocated_tokens_left += amount_in_base_units;

    Ok(())
}
    </related_function>

#[derive(Accounts)]
#[instruction(category_seed: String, amount_in_whole_sbts: u64)]
pub struct DepositCategoryTokens<'info> {
    #[account(mut, signer)]
    pub sender: Signer<'info>,
    #[account(
        mut,
        associated_token::mint = mint,
        associated_token::authority = sender,
        associated_token::token_program = token_program
    )]
    pub sender_ata: InterfaceAccount<'info, TokenAccount>,

    #[account(
        mut,
        seeds = [category_seed.as_bytes(), mint.key().as_ref()],
        bump
    )]
    pub category: Account<'info, InvestorCategoryData>,

    #[account(
        mut,
        associated_token::mint = mint,
        associated_token::authority = category,
        associated_token::token_program = token_program
    )]
    pub category_ata: InterfaceAccount<'info, TokenAccount>,

    pub mint: Box<InterfaceAccount<'info, Mint>>,
    pub token_program: Program<'info, Token2022>,
    pub associated_token_program: Program<'info, AssociatedToken>,
    pub system_program: Program<'info, System>,
}
#[derive(Debug, InitSpace)]
#[account]
pub struct InvestorCategoryData {
    pub monthly_allocation: u64,

    /// Total category allocation minus total allocations of all investors.
    /// Used as the upper limit of allocation when adding new investors.

    use anchor_lang::prelude::*;
use anchor_spl::{
    associated_token::AssociatedToken,
    token_2022::{self, Token2022, TransferChecked},
    token_interface::{Mint, TokenAccount},
};

use crate::{
    states::{investor_category_seed_is_valid, InvestorCategoryData},
    SBT_DECIMALS,
};

pub fn deposit_category_tokens<'info>(
    ctx: Context<'_, '_, '_, 'info, DepositCategoryTokens<'info>>,
    category_seed: String,
    amount_in_whole_sbts: u64,
) -> Result<()> {
    require!(
        investor_category_seed_is_valid(&category_seed),
        crate::error::ErrorCode::CategorySeed
    );

    let Some(amount_in_base_units) =
        amount_in_whole_sbts.checked_mul(10u64.pow(SBT_DECIMALS as u32))
    else {
        msg!("You are depositing WAY too many tokens. Do you know what you're doing?");
        msg!("The instruction expects the amount to be in whole SBTs, not base units!");
        return err!(crate::error::ErrorCode::TooManyTokensAllocated);
    };

    let category = &mut ctx.accounts.category;

    // NOTE: I'm not really sure if this should be constrained in any way.
    // Even the master signature is not necessary. Anybody could allocate tokens to the category,
    // knowing that the master wallet will manage them from that point on.

    require_gte!(
        ctx.accounts.sender_ata.amount,
        amount_in_base_units,
        crate::error::ErrorCode::BalanceInsufficient
    );

    let cpi_accounts = TransferChecked {
        from: ctx.accounts.sender_ata.to_account_info(),
        to: ctx.accounts.category_ata.to_account_info(),
        authority: ctx.accounts.sender.to_account_info(),
        mint: ctx.accounts.mint.to_account_info(),
    };
    let cpi_ctx = CpiContext::new(ctx.accounts.token_program.to_account_info(), cpi_accounts);
    token_2022::transfer_checked(cpi_ctx, amount_in_base_units, SBT_DECIMALS)?;
    category.unallocated_tokens_left += amount_in_base_units;

    Ok(())
}

result.reason: The “non-canonical category_ata” bypass is incorrect: Anchor’s `#[account(associated_token::mint = mint, associated_token::authority = category, associated_token::token_program = token_program)]` constraint validates that the provided account is the canonical ATA for (category, mint, token_program), so an arbitrary token account owned by the category PDA cannot be substituted. The “fee-on-transfer Token-2022” desync is also not supported by this codebase as written: the project’s mint is created via `initialize_mint` as a plain Token-2022 mint (no TransferFeeConfig/deflationary extension initialization is present), so `transfer_checked` should credit the full `amount_in_base_units`, keeping `unallocated_tokens_left` in sync with the ATA balance. This would only become an issue if the protocol intentionally deployed/used a mint with transfer-fee/deflationary extensions, which is not evidenced in the provided code/flows .

invalid_rules_check.prompt:
You are the best security expert in web3 security auditing. 
    Your task is to check whether a given security finding matches any known invalid finding patterns and rules.
    
    **Instructions and Requirements**:
    1. Carefully analyze the finding and compare it against every single rule in the provided InvalidFindingRules.
    2. If a match is found, provide a concise explanation of why the finding is invalid based on the matched rule and set the verdict field to be "invalid".
    3. If no match is found, set the verdict field to "n/a" and set the reason field to "No invalid rules matched, further investigation needed".
    4. You can terminate the analysis early if you find a rule that matches the finding and hence marked the finding as invalid.
    5. Be strict: vague or speculative matches should be excluded. That is, only mark the finding as invalid if it clearly and directly falls under the rule's description. 
    6. You must check against each rule in the InvalidFindingRules list one by one, until a match is found or all rules have been checked.

    ** Output **
    Your output should be an instance of the AIRawFindingVerificationV2 class, which contains the following fields:
    - verdict:  A string indicating the verdict of the finding. At this stage, you can only set it to "invalid" if the finding matches any of the InvalidFindingRules or "n/a" if the finding does not match any of the InvalidFindingRules.
    - confidence: A string indicating the confidence level of the verdict. It can be "Strong", "Medium", or "Weak".
    - reason: A string explaining the reason for the verdict. It should be concise and clear in less than 5 sentences.
    - model: Do not set this field. Leave it empty.

    ** Input **
    Input 1. A **finding** in JSON format to be checked as delimited by <finding> tag.
    <finding>
    {'title': 'Category accounting credits the requested amount instead of the net amount actually received', 'description': 'Root cause & mechanism
The protocol’s category token accounting can desynchronize from real on-chain token balances because deposit and withdrawal logic assumes a 1:1 relationship between the amount requested/recorded and the amount that is actually present and spendable in the category’s canonical associated token account (ATA).

Two concrete mechanisms create this desynchronization:

1) Gross-amount accounting on Token-2022 fee/deflationary transfers
In deposit_category_tokens, the program transfers amount_in_base_units via a Token-2022 CPI (token_2022::transfer_checked) and then blindly credits internal accounting with the same gross amount:
- category.unallocated_tokens_left += amount_in_base_units

For Token-2022 mints with the Transfer Fee extension enabled (TransferFeeConfig with non-zero fee), transfer_checked withholds a fee so the sender is debited amount_in_base_units while the recipient token account (category_ata) is credited only (amount_in_base_units - fee). This inflates InvestorCategoryData.unallocated_tokens_left relative to the actual increase in category_ata.amount (i.e., it records the requested amount instead of the net amount actually received). The same class of issue can occur with other deflationary mechanics such as burns or external balance reductions.

2) Non-canonical category_ata acceptance (mint/owner-only checks) causing “deposit to wrong account”
The deposit_category_tokens account constraints specify:
- associated_token::mint = mint
- associated_token::authority = category
- associated_token::token_program = token_program
but do not use init nor an explicit address = get_associated_token_address(...) check to ensure the passed category_ata is the canonically derived ATA. As described, in this configuration Anchor may only validate that the supplied token account’s mint and owner (authority) match, not that it is the canonical ATA address.

This allows a user to manually create an arbitrary Token-2022 token account owned by the category PDA (via Token-2022 InitializeAccount), pass it as category_ata, and successfully transfer tokens into that alternative account. The program still increments category.unallocated_tokens_left, but other protocol flows “inherently rely on the true canonical ATA to distribute funds”, leading to internal ledger inflation without providing liquidity where later distribution expects it.

Trigger conditions / assumptions
- deposit_category_tokens:
  - category_seed must pass require!(investor_category_seed_is_valid(&category_seed), ErrorCode::CategorySeed) (valid seeds include ["preseed","seed","institutional","vgp","founders"]).
  - amount_in_whole_sbts > 0 and conversion succeeds: amount_in_base_units = amount_in_whole_sbts * 10^SBT_DECIMALS (otherwise ErrorCode::TooManyTokensAllocated).
  - Sender has sufficient balance: require_gte!(ctx.accounts.sender_ata.amount, amount_in_base_units, ErrorCode::BalanceInsufficient).
  - No privileged role is required for deposit; code comments explicitly note “Even the master signature is not necessary. Anybody could allocate tokens to the category…”, so any unprivileged caller can create/expand the mismatch.
  - Desync can be induced by:
    - Using a Token-2022 mint that charges a transfer fee (TransferFeeConfig non-zero), and/or
    - Supplying a non-canonical token account owned by the category PDA as category_ata, and/or
    - Other balance-altering mechanics (e.g., deflationary token mechanics, burns, or direct transfers) that change category token balances without corresponding state updates.

- withdraw_category_tokens:
  - Withdrawal is performed by master (Signer with constraint master.key() == SBARTER_MULTISIG unless LOCAL_TESTING).
  - category_seed must be valid; amount_in_base_units conversion must succeed.
  - Requires:
    - category.unallocated_tokens_left >= amount_in_base_units (ErrorCode::TooManyTokensAllocated)
    - category_ata.amount.saturating_sub(category.tokens_ready_for_claim) >= amount_in_base_units (ErrorCode::TokensUnavailable)
    - category.cliff_started_at != 0 (ErrorCode::TgeNotHappened)

Impact
- Internal ledger inflation / phantom liquidity:
  InvestorCategoryData.unallocated_tokens_left (documented as “Total category allocation minus total allocations of all investors” and “Used as the upper limit of allocation when adding new investors”) can exceed the actual spendable tokens held where the system expects them (the category’s canonical ATA).

- Systemic underfunding/insolvency and denial of service:
  Downstream logic that trusts unallocated_tokens_left as an allocation/withdrawal bound (explicitly mentioned: add_investor_to_category and withdraw_category_tokens; additionally, investor claiming flows) can authorize allocations or withdrawals not fully backed by real balances. Later, when investors attempt to claim vested allocations or when withdrawals/claims execute real Token-2022 transfers, these transfers can fail due to insufficient funds in the expected category token account, effectively “bricking” or DoSing category distribution.

- Withdrawal can consume investors’ vesting tokens under desynced state:
  withdraw_category_tokens tries to protect claimable tokens by limiting withdrawals to category_ata.amount - tokens_ready_for_claim, but category_ata.amount conceptually includes:
  unallocated_tokens + tokens_ready_for_claim + vesting_tokens (allocated for future but not yet ready).
  By subtracting only tokens_ready_for_claim, the second bound treats vesting_tokens as withdrawable. If unallocated_tokens_left is disjointed from the true unallocated balance (e.g., due to transfer fees, burns, or direct transfers), the first check may no longer correctly represent the real unallocated portion; a withdrawal that passes both checks can effectively “eat into” vesting_tokens, permanently depriving investors of future allocations.

Exploitation / reproduction notes (PoC highlights)
A) Fee-on-transfer (Token-2022 TransferFeeConfig) deposit inflation
1. Use or deploy a Token-2022 mint configured with the TransferFeeConfig extension and a non-zero transfer fee.
2. Call deposit_category_tokens with:
   - a valid category_seed,
   - amount_in_whole_sbts large enough to be meaningful,
   - sender_ata funded with at least amount_in_base_units.
3. Observe that token_2022::transfer_checked debits sender_ata by amount_in_base_units but credits category_ata by only (amount_in_base_units - fee).
4. The program still executes category.unallocated_tokens_left += amount_in_base_units, creating a growing mismatch between recorded availability and real tokens.
5. Repeat to amplify the discrepancy; later allocations/withdrawals/claims may be authorized based on the inflated state and then fail or underfund investors.

B) Deposit into non-canonical token account owned by category PDA (ATA-address not enforced)
1. Generate a new Solana keypair to represent an arbitrary token account address.
2. Call Token-2022 InitializeAccount to initialize that address as a token account for the project mint, setting its owner to the category PDA.
3. Fund sender_ata with the project tokens.
4. Invoke deposit_category_tokens, passing the attacker-created token account as category_ata.
5. The checks pass because the provided token account has the correct mint and is owned by the category PDA.
6. Tokens are transferred into the alternative account, while category.unallocated_tokens_left is increased as if liquidity were added to the canonical ATA.
7. Once allocations are made, claims/withdrawal flows that pull from the canonical category ATA can revert due to insufficient balance, causing DoS/insolvency.

C) Vesting-token depletion during withdrawal under desync (illustrative numeric example)
1. Assume category_ata holds 1,000 tokens: 100 unallocated, 200 ready for claim, 700 vesting.
2. Due to an external burn/deflationary fee, category_ata.amount drops to 950 while state remains: unallocated_tokens_left = 100, tokens_ready_for_claim = 200 (vesting implied 700).
3. Call withdraw_category_tokens for 100 tokens.
4. First check passes (100 <= unallocated_tokens_left=100).
5. Second check passes (100 <= 950 - 200 = 750), because vesting tokens are implicitly counted as available.
6. Withdrawal succeeds; category_ata becomes 850. After reserving 200 ready-for-claim, only 650 remain for 700 vesting, meaning the withdrawal effectively consumed 50 tokens that should have backed vesting allocations.

Although the finding involves Token-2022 transfer fees, it is not merely about a fee-on-transfer event/value discrepancy (InvalidFindingRules #4), but about internal accounting desynchronization leading to potential insolvency/DoS. The described issue also does not rely solely on privileged roles or operator mistakes covered by other invalidity rules.
